elements.sent-2411-001: In this work, we attempted to extend the thought and showcase a way forward for the Self-supervised Learning (SSL) learning paradigm by combining contrastive learning, self-distillation (knowledge distillation) and masked data modelling, the three major SSL frameworks, to learn a joint and coordinated representation.
elements.sent-2411-002: The proposed technique of SSL learns by the collaborative power of different learning objectives of SSL.
elements.sent-2411-003: Hence to jointly learn the different SSL objectives we proposed a new SSL architecture KDC-MAE, a complementary masking strategy to learn the modular correspondence, and a weighted way to combine them coordinately.
elements.sent-2411-004: Experimental results conclude that the contrastive masking correspondence along with the KD learning objective has lent a hand to performing better learning for multiple modalities over multiple tasks.
elements.sent-2411-005: A natural tendency of human beings is to learn about the environment by themselves or be self-taught''.
elements.sent-2411-006: Since our childhood, we start learning via supervised training.
elements.sent-2411-007: Slowly we start analyzing and learning things by ourselves or start getting ``self-taught''.
elements.sent-2411-008: There are several aspects by which we get self-taught and a combination of them builds our life.
elements.sent-2411-009: Even the learning representation community adopted the self-taught~\cite{raina2007self as SSL.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Learning without training typically involves recalling the data, grouping similar things and unrelated separately for learning the abstract knowledge from data.
elements.sent-2411-011: Common SSL learning objectives are pretext task~\cite{lee2021predicting , contrastive learning~\cite{jaiswal2020survey , and teacher-student methods~\cite{bucci2021self, schiappa2023self, wang2021knowledge .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: For pretext learning on images one popular task was colorization~\cite{iizuka2016let, larsson2016learning, larsson2017colorization and jigsaw puzzle solving~\cite{noroozi2016unsupervised, kim2018learning, noroozi2018boosting, carlucci2019domain, wei2019iterative, salehi2020puzzle, ren2023masked .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the past, the jigsaw puzzle rearrangement has been used both on spatial and temporal domains for learning better video representations~\cite{wang2022video .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Various works~\cite{srivastava2015unsupervised, vondrick2016generating, tulyakov2018mocogan, wang2022bevt used video reconstruction as learning objectives by disentangling motion and content, using scene dynamics and transformers.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Super-resolution is gaining popularity as a learning objective for scale-independent generalized learning~\cite{xia2023structured .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=14cm]{graphic_abstract.pdf} \caption{Proposed improvement on existing SSL by complementary mask and self-distillation. The model uses shared weights with two masked versions of the same audio-video pair passed through the encoder, generating separate joint latent embeddings. KL divergence loss aligns these embeddings, followed by a joint decoder that splits audio and video, with contrastive loss applied to the latent embeddings.} \label{fig:graphic_abstract}
elements.sent-2411-016: Contrastive learning is a widely used learning objective.
elements.sent-2411-017: A comprehensive survey~\cite{feichtenhofer2021large shows that approaches can be classified into three categories.}
elements.sent-2411-018: First, Binary classification of ``good pair'' and ``bad pair'' using Binary Cross Entropy (BCE) loss~\cite{knights2021temporally, yao2021seco .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Second, SSL can be done with discriminators, which is the fuzzy version of the binary classification~\cite{tao2020self, wang2021enhancing .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Third, the most popular approach in SSL is to use Noise-Contrastive Estimation (NCE) loss~\cite{gutmann2010noise to pretrain the model by pulling similar data pairs closer and pushing dissimilar data pairs further.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Though originally devised for Natural language Processing (NLP) tasks~\cite{mnih2013learning , the concept is now widely used in video~\cite{han2020self, lorre2020temporal, qian2021spatiotemporal, yuan2022contextualized, kordopatis2023self , images~\cite{chaitanya2020contrastive, nguyen2023joint, denize2023similarity and audio~\cite{liu2022audio .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In recent years since Masked Autoencoders (MAE) was proposed for vision tasks~\cite{he2022masked , the domain has been heavily explored by researchers resulting in a huge amount of research outcomes in recent years.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Due to huge interest in this area, comprehensive surveys~\cite{zhang2022survey,zhang2023survey, zhou2023masked were made in recent times.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Subsequently, video-MAE~\cite{tong2022videomae,feichtenhofer2022masked, wei2022masked , Audio-visual MAE\cite{georgescu2023audiovisual and CAV-MAE~\cite{gong2022contrastive are proposed on this direction of research.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: In recent time-motion-guided masking~\cite{yang2022self, huang2023mgmae and dual masking~\cite{wang2023videomae have been explored in this regard.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: It is observed that the selection of a masking policy has a profound impact on the learnability of models.
elements.sent-2411-027: Knowledge distillation also plays an important role in self-distillation between instances with different masking policies~\cite{chen2022sdae, zhang2022mask .}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is also observed that knowledge distillation can provide a significant boost to SSL~\cite{zhang2018deep, grill2020bootstrap, caron2021emerging, oquab2023dinov2 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The aforementioned learning objectives of SSL are well explored individually and found to perform nonuniformly for different scenarios when they are employed individually.
elements.sent-2411-030: It is expected that they will work better if they learn jointly to find mutual correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-031: A recent work \cite{gong2022contrastive attempted to unify the concepts of masked autoencoder for the reconstruction of video frames and contrastive learning between video and audio tokens to learn better multimodal representations.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The reconstruction task of CAV-MAE forces its representation to encode the input information in the fusion and the constructive task helps to find the explicit audio-visual correspondence objective but lacks in finding the modular correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-033: This motivates us to propose Knowledge Distilled Contrastive Mask Auto-Encoder (KDC-MAE), an optimized manner to use modular knowledge distillation along with masked data modelling, and inter-modal contrastive learning to learn joint multimodal correspondence of audio and video for unified learning representation.
elements.sent-2411-034: We proposed to use dual complementary masks as input to dual head mask-autoencoder in a weight-sharing fashion to add modular correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-035: Further, to foster the modular correspondence, Kullback–Leibler divergence (KL) of the representation of the dual mask of each modality is computed in the encoding space and propagated as a loss (See Fig.~\ref{fig:graphic_abstract ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: From our experiments, we conclude that these learning objectives can boost learning in an optimal combination.
elements.sent-2411-037: We demonstrate our findings through various ablation studies at the pretraining level.
elements.sent-2411-038: To support our claim we utilized the pre-trained models in further several downstream tasks over different modalities and their combinations, on which we obtained better results.
elements.sent-2411-040: To propose that three paradigms of SSL namely contrastive learning, masked data modelling, and knowledge distillation objectives are not complementary for unified joint modelling.
elements.sent-2411-041: Introduction of complementary masking strategy for finding the modular correspondence of the modalities.
elements.sent-2411-042: Introduction of static regularizers as weights for equating the various losses of the different tasks of SSL for joint and collaborative learning to generalise the contextual information extraction capability of the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-043: In our proposed work we focused on optimization of the ensemble of the SSL, few questions arose for the training approach: (a) Investigating whether reconstruction from masked input, contrastive learning and knowledge distillation is complementary; (b) If they are not complementary, in what way can they be best combined? (b) How can we incorporate mutual learning as a form of self distillation~\cite{zhang2018deep , with different unmasked tokens as input to weight shared instances of the encoder, to train it better? (d) How can we make the SSL learning task tougher by experimenting with more masking strategies in order to make the encoder learn better?.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We follow a similar technique for pre-processing and tokenization as in MAE \cite{dosovitskiy2020image which was further adopted by CAV-MAE ~\cite{gong2022contrastive .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Hence, we take on audio-visual data by considering a frame of the $10$sec video clip as the visual input and the Mel Spectrogram of the entire audio as an image, as the audio input.
elements.sent-2411-046: The encoder architecture for both modalities is the same and the decoder is common.
elements.sent-2411-047: The heart of the encoder is based on transformer layers~\cite{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Both the audio encoder(AE) and video encoder(VE) are composed of $11$ transformer layers and the joint audio-video encoder(AVE) contains $1$ layer.
elements.sent-2411-049: The total $12$ layer encoder is based on the ViT base model~\cite{dosovitskiy2020image .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Thus its input is also $16 \times 16$ patches, termed as unmasked tokens.
elements.sent-2411-051: The video input frames are scaled-center-cropped $224 \times 224$ thus resulting in $196$ patches.
elements.sent-2411-052: The audio spectrogram is of size $128 \times 1024$ (features $\times$ time) and thus it results in $512$ $16 \times 16$ patches.
elements.sent-2411-053: The masking ratio was kept at $75\%$ and thus $49$ video patches and $128$ audio patches were the input for the audio and the video encoder.
elements.sent-2411-054: After passing through the $11$ layers of both the embeddings are concatenated and passed through the single layer of the audio-video encoder.
elements.sent-2411-055: Three sets of embeddings, i.e. the video, audio and audio video are passed in three consecutive passes to keep the time complexity in check.
elements.sent-2411-056: The audio and video embeddings of the ith sample ($a_i,v_i$) are subjected to meanpool operation to compute ($c^v_i,c^a_j)$ for further calculation of contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The concatenated embeddings ($\mathbf{x^\prime}$) are passed through the joint decoder (JD) which has $16$ attention heads and has $8$ layers.
elements.sent-2411-058: Both audio and video embeddings pass through common $7$ layers of JD and the last modality-specific layer for final patch reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-059: The masked tokens are passed as trainable masked tokens, $16 \times 16$ patches attached to the computation graph, during the process.
elements.sent-2411-060: Each reconstructed token gets into its proper place due to modality-specific sinusoidal position embedding.
elements.sent-2411-061: The idea of using zero-valued patches is best explained in~\cite{cao2022understand .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{r}$ is computed between the masked patches ($75\%$ of the video frame and the spectrogram).}
elements.sent-2411-063: The equations for $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$ and $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{r}$ are as follows: \begin{equation}\label{equ:contrastive} \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{c} = - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N {\rm log} \left[ \frac{ {\rm exp} (s_{i,i}/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} {\rm exp} (s_{i,k}/\tau) + {\rm exp} (s_{i,i}/\tau)} \right]\end{equation} where $s_{i,j = \|c^v_i\|^T\|c^a_j\|$ and $\tau$ is a temperature parameter. \begin{equation}\hat{\mathbf{a}}_i, \hat{\mathbf{v}}_i = \mathrm{D}(\mathbf{x^\prime} + [\mathbf{E_a^\prime}, \mathbf{E_v^\prime}] + [\mathbf{E^p_a}^\prime, \mathbf{E^p_v}^\prime])\end{equation} \begin{equation} \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{r} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \left[\frac{\sum(\hat{\mathbf{a}}_i^{\mu}-\mathrm{norm}(\mathbf{a}_i^{\mu}))^2}{|\mathbf{a}_i^{\mu}|} + \frac{\sum(\hat{\mathbf{v}}_i^{\mu}-\mathrm{norm}(\mathbf{v}_i^{\mu}))^2}{|\mathbf{v}_i^{\mu}|}\right]\end{equation} where $N$ is the mini-batch size; $\mathbf{a ^{\mu}$, $\mathbf{v ^{\mu}$, $\hat{\mathbf{a ^{\mu}$, $\hat{\mathbf{v ^{\mu}$ denote the original and predicted masked patches; $|\mathbf{a ^{\mu |$ and $|\mathbf{v ^{\mu |$ denote the number of masked audio and visual patches, $\mathrm{D()}$ is the joint decoder (JD), $\mathbf{E_a^\prime}$ and $\mathbf{E_v^\prime}$ are modality type embedding (for $\mathrm{D()}$ to recognize audio and video tokens), $\mathbf{E^p_a ^\prime$ and $\mathbf{E^p_v ^\prime$ are modality specific 2D sinusoidal embeddings, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{equ:contrastive} L_c = - \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N {\rm log} \left[ \frac{ {\rm exp} (s_{i,i}/\tau)}{\sum_{k \neq i} {\rm exp} (s_{i,k}/\tau) + {\rm exp} (s_{i,i}/\tau)} \right]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-064: The final loss is computed as $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{total = \mathcal{L _\mathrm{r + \lambda_c\cdot\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$ where $\lambda_c$ is a regulating parameter empirically chosen as $0.01$.}}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Complementary mask: why, what and how?
elements.sent-2411-065: There are a few important things that we observed revisiting the closely related previous works and the reason why we proposed complementary mask: $\mathcal{L_\mathrm{c}$} only does positive pair mining, i.e. pull together corresponding audio and video.
elements.sent-2411-066: Thus there is no way to find the correspondence between the modality itself, which is important as we are heavily sampling both the audio and video before encoding.
elements.sent-2411-067: This motivates us to use self-distillation between modality.
elements.sent-2411-068: Due to the nature of implementation, the JD is unaware of the contrastive learning effect.
elements.sent-2411-069: This measure is necessary as the decoder will be discarded for downstream tasks and thus its only concern should be how to reconstruct the embeddings to the masked input patches, the only possible annotation available for for SSL.
elements.sent-2411-070: Thus self distillation should be done in the encoded space like constructive task.
elements.sent-2411-071: The masking strategy is random and thus there is no implied correspondence between the video and audio tokens, that are passing through VE and AE respectively.
elements.sent-2411-072: As it is impossible to devise any temporal correlation between random spatial patches of a single video frame and the total audio spectrogram, thus self-distillation between video and audio to find the modular correspondence directly is impossible in the present form of data representation.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, distillation between individual modalities of audio and video and the joint embeddings makes sense as the couples belong to the same modality/category.
elements.sent-2411-074: Thus to materialise the same the duel head MAE is employed with non-overlapping or overlapping complementary patches as input to each heads.
elements.sent-2411-075: The concept of the complementary mask is simply the generation of two masks so that $M_1$ and $M_2$ are generated as sets of unmasked tokens in such a way that the intersection of them is a null set i.e $M_1 \cap M_2 = \phi$.
elements.sent-2411-076: The concept is pictorially described in Fig.~\ref{fig:approach .}
elements.sent-2411-077: It should be noted that $n$ complementary random masks are only possible for a masking ratio $m_r \geq (n/N)$ where $N$ is the total number of input patches.
elements.sent-2411-078: In our case, $n=2$ thus $m_r \geq 0.5$ will suffice.
elements.sent-2411-079: As the masking ratio is $0.75$ thus there was no need to change the masking ratio.
elements.sent-2411-080: At first, a normal random mask is generated with masking ratio $m_r$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Let the set of all tokens for audio modality be $U_a=512$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Thus if the first mask $M_1^a$ is considered as a set then the residue tokens left for consideration in $M_2^a$ is $U_a - M_1^a$, thus the set $M_2^a$ is randomly chosen from that residual set so that $|M_1^a| = |M_2^a| = m_r\cdot |U_a|$ and $M_1^a \cap M_2^a = \phi$.
elements.sent-2411-083: Similar masks $M_1^v$ and $M_2^v$ are generated for the video modality from $U_v$ where $|U_v|=196$.
elements.sent-2411-084: It should be noted that there is no relation imposed between the audio and video masks while generating the complementary mask.
elements.sent-2411-085: The motivation behind complementary patches is making the embedding mask agnostic to find modal correspondence, i.e. if the embeddings are treated as probability distributions, they should be closer irrespective of the input tokens, provided that they are from the same data point.
elements.sent-2411-086: After all a masked encoder is supposed to be a function $F(x)$ where $x$ is a subset of the input $X$ in the form of randomly chosen patches.
elements.sent-2411-087: Thus if two subsets $x_1$ and $x_2$ are there such as $x_1 \subset X, x_2 \subset X, x_1 \cap x_2=\phi$ are there, $F()$ should be trained in such a way that $F(x_1) \simeq F(x_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-088: As $X$ will be reconstructed from $x_1$ and $x_2$ by a single decoder which is shallower than the encoder, it is the task of the encoder to incorporate the entire information of all patches in the unmasked patches.
elements.sent-2411-089: Thus the best encoder is one that can generate similar input to the decoder irrespective of the input subset, even if the two subsets have no overlap at all.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Learning strategy of the proposed SSL-model
elements.sent-2411-090: Gong et al.~\cite{gong2022contrastive already proved different learning strategies of SSL are not complementary.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Their work demonstrated that a combination of contrastive and reconstruction loss works well towards learning a generic multimodal encoder for SSL.
elements.sent-2411-092: However, they have not explored all combinations of SSL strategies to optimize their combination.
elements.sent-2411-093: Hence, we introduced in this context self-distillation along with contrastive-mask encoding.
elements.sent-2411-094: The idea of self-distillations works like contrastive learning here but the contrast is between two forms of the same input across all modalities and their combinations, rather than just two modalities of the same input.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the reconstruction loss, the accuracy of the masked token reconstruction is checked and is trained to the patches of the masked input patches with the help of information provided in the JD, by the encoded audio-visual embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-096: Our goal is to force the embeddings to preserve better information so that it can provide that to the trainable masked token in the JD, in turn improving the power of the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-097: DINO is a popular model to start from when it comes to self-distillation with no labels~\cite{caron2021emerging .}
elements.sent-2411-098: But at any one point in time, it is unidirectional in nature.
elements.sent-2411-099: An older approach called Deep Mutual Learning(DML)~\cite{zhang2018deep appeared to be a better candidate for our consideration after initial experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-100: We tuned the nature of DML by redesigning the training methodology with masked data modelling in mind.
elements.sent-2411-101: For that, we wagered our approach on a symmetric form of KL divergence.
elements.sent-2411-102: The Kullback-Leibler (KL) distance (discrete version) is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}(p_1||p_2) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1}(p_1(i)log\frac{p_1(i)}{p_2(i)})\end{equation} where $p_1$ and $p_2$ are two probability distributions of $n$ distinct samples.
elements.sent-2411-103: In terms of latent embedding the $n$ translates to the dimension size.
elements.sent-2411-104: Though KL divergence provides a very good log-likelihood measure of two distributions, the major caveat is its asymmetric property i.e. $\mathcal{D (p_1||p_2) \neq \mathcal{D (p_2||p_1)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Also, the inputs must be a probability distribution or $\sum_{i=0 ^{n-1 p(i) = 1$ and $p(i)\geq 0 \, \forall \, i$ which may not be the case for an embedding.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The embeddings between which the KL divergence is measured are the mean pool for all audio and video tokens that will be subjected to pass to JD (without the positional encoding).
elements.sent-2411-107: In our case we meanpool $\mathbf{x^\prime}$ to $\mathbf{\chi}$ where $n=|\mathbf{\chi |=768$ as the patches have dimension $16 \times 16$ with three channels.}
elements.sent-2411-108: To get the embedding for KL divergence we first find the minimum value $\mathbf{\chi _{min}$ in the embedding vector ($\mathbf{\chi}$) and then sum or subtract that to the vector, i.e. $\mathbf{\chi =\, <\mathbf{\chi (i) (+/-)|\mathbf{\chi _{min |, \, \forall \, i>$, depending on whether $\mathbf{\chi _{min}$ is negative or positive, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: This is done to make sure $\mathbf{\chi (i) \geq 0 \, \forall i$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Then $\mathbf{\chi}$ is converted to a probability distribution by simple linear normalization i.e. $p = (\mathbf{\chi (i)/\sum_{j=0 ^{n-1}\mathbf{\chi (j)), \forall \, i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: Something as common as softmax is avoided as that is tuned for classification problems, which exaggerates the divergence of the distribution for the sake of clarity.
elements.sent-2411-112: Our purpose is not classification but a near-accurate projection of $\mathbf{\chi}$ into a probability distribution $p$ so that KL-divergence can be applied.
elements.sent-2411-113: Now to eliminate the asymmetric effect of KL divergence, we use the self-distillation loss ($\mathcal{L _{kd}$) formula: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{kd} (p_1,p_2)= \frac{\mathcal{D}(p_1||p_2) + \mathcal{D}(p_2||p_1)}{2}\end{equation} Where $p_1$ and $p_2$ are the probability distribution projection of the origin corrected mean-pooled audio-video embeddings $\mathbf{\chi _{1}$ and $\mathbf{\chi _{2}$ coming from the parallel streams (refer Fig.~\ref{fig:our_approach ) with input masks $M_1$ and $M_2$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: If we consider $\mathbf{x^\prime}$ to be the probability distribution in a translated latent space $\mathbf{L^\prime \in \Re^{n}$ then a properly learnt encoder $F(x)$ should compute $\mathbf{x^\prime_1 = F(x_1)$ and $\mathbf{x^\prime_2 =F(x_2)$ so that $\mathbf{x^\prime_1 \simeq \mathbf{x^\prime_2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: For MAE, the decoder $D()$ is getting trained such as $D(\mathbf{x^\prime_1 ) = D(\mathbf{x^\prime_2 ) = X$.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: As $D()$ is to be discarded, only the condition $\mathbf{x^\prime_1 \simeq \mathbf{x^\prime_2}$ can ensure such an outcome.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Thus we chose to propagate the $\mathcal{L _{kd}$ only through $F()$, to make it powerful without being dependent on $D()$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: As it is proven~\cite{anderson2004model that model selection can be best measured with KL divergence from the ground truth distribution, in our case, due to lack of ground truth in $\mathbf{L^\prime}$, mutual ground truth is the only option left.}
elements.sent-2411-119: The idea is that whatever may be the true latent representation, the encoder should be able to approximate a distribution near that, irrespective of input.
elements.sent-2411-120: MAE~\cite{he2022masked has already proved that random masking solves this purpose, thus making the encoder learn better.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Hence, complementary inputs will make this divergence, as at the encoder level there is no option for information exchange between the two sets of complementary tokens.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Combining all the losses and learning objectives
elements.sent-2411-122: We have discussed about three losses, this is how to translate to different learning objectives.
elements.sent-2411-123: Contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$ translates to the learning objective ``audio and video of the same datapoint should be closer''.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{r}$ translates to ``even with masked input, the deep encoder should extract enough information to use a shallow decoder for reconstructing the total input''.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Self Distillation loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd}$ translates to ``whatever may be the mask, the latent audio-video representation of a datapoint should not vary.'' A scalar factor $\lambda_{c =0.01$ for the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$ and $\lambda_{kd =10$ for self-distillation loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd}$ was used before adding to the reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{r}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: Due to the nature of implementation, during backward pass $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{r}$ affects the modality-specific encoders, the joint encoder and the joint decoder but the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{c}$ and $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd $only effects later two.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: This is due to the way the graph updates weights based on the local gradient computed in the forward pass and the downstream gradient computed in the backward pass.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=120mm]{comp_mask.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{combined.pdf} \caption{} \end{subfigure} \caption{(a) The complementary mask generation. Note: Two video masks and audio masks are complementary but there is no relation between any two audio and video masks, (b) The proposed architecture of KDC-MAE: (Symbol index on the right)} \label{fig:approach}
elements.sent-2411-128: The self-distillation loss $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd}$ works best for two mutual teacher/student (stream) configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-129: For more parallel streams, the performance degrades as proven by experiments.
elements.sent-2411-130: If more than two streams are used then $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd}$ is taken as the mean of all three steams i.e. $\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd (p_1,p_2, p_3) = (\mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd (p_1,p_2) + \mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd (p_1,p_3) + \mathcal{L _\mathrm{kd (p_2,p_3))/3$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: In such a configuration the the divergence between two streams affects the third stream, to which it has no contribution.
elements.sent-2411-132: Though the higher number of student cohorts of the parallel stream is supposed to enhance accuracy~\cite{zhang2018deep the trend reverses due to too much exposure to data as 3 complementary masks expose $75\%$ of the data, which overfits the model.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Thus it is proven that a complementary masking strategy is not the holy grail when it comes to training an MAE.
elements.sent-2411-134: We performed extensive experiments and ablation studies with different learning objectives combined in different ways.
elements.sent-2411-135: Our final configuration is a result of incremental change in various key factors and those results are presented in an ordered fashion to elaborate our path of analysis.
elements.sent-2411-136: We have used $4$ datasets for SSL pretraining and modality-specific finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-137: The downstream task in finetuning has been only classification across different modalities of data.
elements.sent-2411-138: The major two datasets that we worked with are AudioSet~\cite{gemmeke2017audio and VGGsound~\cite{chen2020VGGsound for training and testing the methodology on audio-visual modality.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: We have used the Kinetics~\cite{kay2017Kinetics dataset for video-only action recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-140: We have not experimented separately on image modality as the video frame reconstruction is the same task.
elements.sent-2411-141: AudioSet~\cite{gemmeke2017audio , is a collection of audio label annotated YouTube clips of $10$ seconds duration.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Due to the unavailability of many videos, the dataset on which we based our experiments has $1472186$ training videos and $7486$ test videos (for label classification).
elements.sent-2411-143: The total number of classes is $527$ that are not distributed in a balanced fashion.
elements.sent-2411-144: Our use of AudioSet was mainly in SSL pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-145: A balanced subset called AudioSet $20$k with $8227$ datapoints is used for finetuning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-146: Like the full Audioset, many videos are missing from the original list, in the subset also.
elements.sent-2411-147: VGGsound~\cite{chen2020VGGsound , is a also collection of audio label annotated YouTube clips of $10$ seconds duration like AudioSet.}
elements.sent-2411-148: For the same reason as AudioSet, we were able to experiment with $164003$ training videos and $13560$ test videos.
elements.sent-2411-149: The total number of classes is $300$ and the samples are not balanced over classes.
elements.sent-2411-150: Kinetics-400~\cite{kay2017Kinetics is also a huge collection of $10$ second YouTube clips where only the video modality is used for human action recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-151: We have used $230$k training videos and $19$k validation and testing videos.
elements.sent-2411-152: ILSVRC2017~\cite{ILSVRC15 is a subset of the ImageNet dataset consisting of 1,281,167 training images, 50,000 validation images and 100,000 test images, all belonging to one of 1000 classes.}
elements.sent-2411-153: For all the dataset numbers may vary from the officially released number due to the aforementioned reasons of working with a collection of YouTube videos.
elements.sent-2411-154: Regarding hardware, we have used multiple A$100{\text - 80$ and A$6000{\text - 48$ cards in PyTorch dataparallel mode~\cite{li2020pytorch .}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: For pretraining we have used a batch size of $120$ and for finetuning it is $48$ for VGGsound and 36 for AudioSet20K.
elements.sent-2411-156: The learning rate starts from $0.00005$ and starts decreasing by half every five epochs, after the $15$th epoch.
elements.sent-2411-157: For finetuning, the learning rate starts from $0.0001$ and is halved every epoch starting from $2$nd epoch.
elements.sent-2411-158: Only a few experiments were done on the much larger AudioSet dataset, due to its sheer size and thus delay in training.
elements.sent-2411-159: In the tables, AudioSet is abbreviated as AS, and VGGsound is abbreviated as VGG.
elements.sent-2411-160: The metric of measurement is classification accuracy in every form of experiment.
elements.sent-2411-161: Table~\ref{tab:vanilla_selfD shows three rows the first being the results of finetuning on different combinations of both datasets VGG sound and AS-20K on the CAV-MAE.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The second one is Moving Average mutual Knowledge Distillation (MAKD)\cite{grill2020bootstrap , where instead of having shared weights in the two streams the one with lesser loss (teacher) propagated its weight to the one with higher loss (student), for a certain iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-163: This role reverses dynamically and thus the ``mutual'' terms come in play.
elements.sent-2411-164: This was our primary attempt towards incorporating KD as the third pillar of SSL after masked data modelling and contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-165: The third shows the best result obtained by our proposed ensemble technique of dual complementary mask and self-distillation-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-166: It can be observed that our ensembled method showed a jump in accuracy over the vanilla technique in the multimodal configuration and the video-only configuration in finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-167: It can also be seen that for AudioSet the proposed method performed better for AV classification.

elements.sent-2411-168: The final loss propagated with our ensembled method is either determined by dynamic weights as learnable parameters during training or by manually assigning the weights $\lambda_{c}$ and $\lambda_{kd}$ for the contrastive and self-distillation losses respectively.
elements.sent-2411-169: As per CAV-MAE authors, $\lambda_{c =0.01$ is an optimal choice as per their study so we kept it unchanged and experimented with various values of $\lambda_{kd}$, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:diff_kd_weights .}}
elements.sent-2411-170: By overlapped and non-overlapped masks, we mean $n$ random masks $M_i$ so that $M_i \cap M_j \stackrel{ {= \phi\,\forall \, i,j,i\neq j$ and $M_i \cap M_j = \phi\,\forall \, i,j,i\neq j$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: We observe that for $\lambda_{kd =10$ with complementary masking, we achieve the best result among all our experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Note that in configurations where VGGsound results were not satisfactory, the experiments were not repeated for AudioSet.
elements.sent-2411-173: In this regard, it must be clarified that we have used the abbreviation ``kd'' for self-distillation.
elements.sent-2411-174: Also, the result of adaptive masking strategy~\cite{bandara2023adamae is compared with our complementary masking strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-175: In that work, the mask is adaptively generated based on the input image.

elements.sent-2411-176: The self-distillation strategy is about measuring the divergence between the latent vectors out of the joint encoder and then propagating that as a loss back through the joint encoder, audio and video encoders.
elements.sent-2411-177: Though while training a multimodal encoder, the joint encoding matters the most, we experimented with the divergences between the outputs of the audio encoders and video encoders of both streams.
elements.sent-2411-178: Our motivation was to study the fact that whether a better joint representation learning can be enhanced by ensuring better modality-specific learning.
elements.sent-2411-179: In Table~\ref{tab:diff_aa_vv we have the results with the configurations of overlapping dual mask and non-overlapping dual mask in row $1$ and $2$ respectively for the different dataset combinations.}
elements.sent-2411-180: We also experimented with the same configurations with dynamic weights replacing $\lambda_{c}$ and $\lambda_{kd}$.
elements.sent-2411-181: In Fig.~\ref{fig:approach , the violet round denotes the loss mixer, which is named so that the mixing can be either done with manual weights or dynamic weights.}
elements.sent-2411-182: The dynamic weights are learnable parameters in the PyTorch computation graph that get updated by the global loss.
elements.sent-2411-183: Row $3$ and $4$ show the results with dynamic weight in Table~\ref{tab:diff_aa_vv , where row $4$ is the configuration with dynamic weight and divergence on just the joint encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-184: It can be observed that dynamic weight delivers better accuracy when distillation between all pairs of encoders (AE, VE, AVE) are considered.

elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Results of different masking strategies on VGG-Sound} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|c|cccc|} \multirow{3}{*}{Model} \multicolumn{4}{c|}{\multirow{2}{*}{Classification Accuracy (\%)}} \multicolumn{4}{c|}{} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Time} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Frequency} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{TF} Uniform CAV-MAE \multicolumn{1}{c|}{63.58} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{63.77} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{63.51} 63.89 With Overlap \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.91} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.90} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.21} 63.98 No Overlap \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.83} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.68} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{64.01} {64.05} \end{tabular}} \label{tab:structured_mask}
elements.sent-2411-185: As discussed in the later part of Section~\ref{sec:combining_losses , the consideration of more than two parallel streams behaves counter-intuitively as proven by experimental results in Table~\ref{tab:triple_stream .}}
elements.sent-2411-186: It can be observed that for three parallel streams, complementary masks perform worse than overlapping masks.
elements.sent-2411-187: The analyzed reason for this behaviour is due to too much data being exposed, especially for the video modality, which overfits the model gradually.
elements.sent-2411-188: For the same reason overlapping masks perform better as the amount of exposed data is bound to be lesser than complementary masks if we take uniform random sampling for keeping unmasked tokens.
elements.sent-2411-189: One interesting observation is that for audioset the triple-stream technique fares better with audio-only finetuning too.
elements.sent-2411-190: The reason is that a larger dataset mitigates overfitting and due to the comprehensive nature of the audio data (spectrogram is for the entire clip, not a single frame sample from a clip of $~300$ frames) the model gets trained better (contradicting videos cannot nullify the knowledge of audio modality).
elements.sent-2411-191: In this regard, it should also be mentioned that the audio-only finetuning fares much better than video-only finetuning as the datasets are annotated for audio and not video.
elements.sent-2411-192: The same can also be observed from the results in Table \ref{tab:other_datasets , which reports the finetuning accuracy on more datasets like ILSRVC, Kinetics-400 and Audioset 20k subset.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Again, it can be seen that classification accuracy only improves when using the proposed dual mask method in all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-194: Another aspect that has been explored is the evaluation of downstream tasks such as retrieval, localization and inpainting as shown in Table \ref{tab:downstream .}
elements.sent-2411-195: It can be seen that the proposed model performs better in the case of audio-to-visual retrieval and the case of inpainting and is quite competitive for localisation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-196: Yet another important observation can be made by comparing the performance improvement with structured masking vs uniform masking as described in Table~\ref{tab:structured_mask .}
elements.sent-2411-197: It can be concluded that uniform unstructured masking provides the best result when compared to time, frequency or time-frequency(TF) based methods.
elements.sent-2411-198: More results on downstream tasks, masking and visualization are reported in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-199: In order to demonstrate the efficacy of using self-distillation along with the complementary masking strategy for SSL pretraining, we have fine-tuned various model configurations on the Kinetics dataset We pre-trained all the encoders (i.e. AE, VE and AVE) on Kinetics and we finetuned for human action recognition downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-200: The model was initiated with AudioSet pre-trained weights as the starting point.
elements.sent-2411-201: The results are 71.90\%, 71.78\% and 71.69\% for no overlap, with overlap and CAVMAE.
elements.sent-2411-202: We also tested on ILSVRC, with the accuracy of 73.73\%, 74.53\% and 74.76\% for CAVMAE, overlap and nonoverlap respectively.
elements.sent-2411-203: These results prove that pretraining with a task-specific dataset makes the encoder learn well (which is obvious!) and self-distillation in some form (complementary masked or overlapping masked input pairs) makes the encoder learn better, unequivocally.
elements.sent-2411-204: It can be observed that the overlapped dual mask performed better than the complementary mask as for Kinetics the annotation is based on video rather than audio, thus overexposure of video modality caused it to overfit.
elements.sent-2411-205: From the experiments conducted, we can summarize our findings about our proposed model in the following points: complementary masks have performed well or generated competitive results depending on the downstream task, especially on multimodal datasets.
elements.sent-2411-206: As the video sampling is not as uniform as audio, complementary masks don't scale well when the downstream annotation is based on video instead.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, for audio-based annotation in downstream tasks, complementary masking scales up the results elegantly.
elements.sent-2411-208: Moreover, the induction of knowledge distillation with KL-divergence on the complementary brings in the modular correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-209: This makes the encoder struggle to learn mask agnostic modelling and the model starts to learn fine modality-specific information.
elements.sent-2411-210: Further, along with the mask encoder that forces encoding the input information in the fusion and the constructive task that helps to find the explicit audio-visual correspondence, the knowledge-distillation helps to find the modular correspondence as results provide better modelling.
elements.sent-2411-211: In the quest for finding an optimal learning strategy for SSL on large datasets, we ended up exploring many untrodden nooks and corners of knowledge distillation and masked data modelling.
elements.sent-2411-212: Masked autoencoders first leveraged the idea of masked data modelling on image modality with reconstruction from incomplete data as the sole learning objective.
elements.sent-2411-213: Later CAV-MAE elevated that for training a task-agnostic multimodal learner by incorporating intermodal contrastive learning as an extra learning objective.
elements.sent-2411-214: In our proposed learning strategy we have demonstrated that adding knowledge distillation, driven by divergence between embeddings from two inputs subjected to complementary masks, elevated the result still further.
elements.sent-2411-215: We have compared our results with the state-of-the-art methodology for the task, CAV-MAE and found that the proposed KDC-MAE achieved better results.
elements.sent-2411-216: This work opened the door for many paths to explore and future versions for more extensive studies.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
